title: Supplementary weft pickup on a backstrap loom
subtitle: Creating patterned supplementary weft floats on a backstrap loom using a pickup stick
excerpt: Supplementary weft pickup produces patterned floats on the surface of a plain weave ground by lifting selected warp ends with a pickup stick before passing the pattern weft. This technique is used across Andean, Southeast Asian, and Central American backstrap loom traditions and produces complex geometric patterns with only a plain weave threading.
sourceNotes: Supplementary weft pickup on backstrap looms synthesised from general backstrap loom weaving practice. The technique is foundational to Andean and Southeast Asian patterned weaving traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: An additional weft thread, usually in a contrasting colour, that floats on the surface of the cloth to create the pattern, while the main ground weft weaves a plain weave base beneath.

A flat, smooth stick used to lift selected warp ends out of the natural shed, creating a new opening through which the supplementary weft passes. The pattern is encoded in the arrangement of lifted warp ends.
A weft thread that passes over more than one consecutive warp end. Floats form the visible pattern in supplementary weft pickup.
The main weft thread that weaves plain weave picks between each supplementary weft pick, forming the foundation cloth that holds the pattern weft in place.

content.text: Supplementary weft pickup creates geometric patterns by adding a second weft thread that
lifts specific warp ends to create a new shed distinct from the plain weave sheds. The pattern weft passes through this pickup shed, while alternating
picks weave plain weave between each pattern row. The result is a
pattern visible on the cloth face, backed by the ground weave.
Step 1: Set up the loom and choose the pattern

Set up the backstrap loom with a plain weave threading at 8-10 ends per cm in a smooth cotton or fine wool warp. Choose a geometric pattern with a clear repeat: a simple stripe of floats over 3 warp ends, then skip 1, is a good first pattern. The total warp width must be a multiple of the pattern repeat. Prepare a separate bobbin or shuttle wound with the supplementary weft yarn in a contrasting colour.

Open the lower shed of the loom (using the heddle bar). Into this shed, insert the pickup stick and use it to lift every third warp end (the pattern ends) onto the upper surface of the stick, passing under the intervening ends. Work across the full warp width following the pattern repeat. Turn the pickup stick on its edge to open a new shed: only the selected pattern ends are now raised. The pickup stick holds this pattern shed open.

With the pickup stick shed open, pass the supplementary weft through. Beat gently. Slide the pickup stick back but do not remove it from the warp: leave it in position to re-open on the next pattern row. Weave 2 ground weft picks in plain weave (upper and lower sheds). Then turn the pickup stick on its edge again and pass the supplementary weft a second time. Repeat: 1 pattern pick, 2 ground picks, 1 pattern pick, 2 ground picks.

To weave a second pattern row that steps the design diagonally, remove the pickup stick and re-pick it for the new row, shifting the selected warp ends by one position relative to the previous row. This stepping creates diagonal or chevron patterns. For a repeated identical pattern, keep the same pickup stick setting for the full length of the piece.

items.problem: The supplementary weft floats are not visible: they disappear into the ground weave
items.cause: The supplementary weft is too similar in diameter or colour to the ground weft
items.fix: Use a clearly contrasting colour and a slightly heavier yarn for the supplementary weft. The pattern weft should sit proud of the ground picks, not blend with them.

items.problem: The pickup stick shed collapses and is difficult to open
items.cause: The warp tension is too low to support the pickup stick cleanly
items.fix: Increase the backstrap tension by leaning back slightly before inserting the pickup stick. A firm warp tension keeps the raised ends clearly separated from the lowered ones.

items.problem: The pattern is irregular across the width
items.cause: The pickup stick selection was inconsistent: some pattern ends were missed or an extra end was lifted
items.fix: Count the pattern repeat carefully across the full warp before turning the stick. A contrasting colour thread tied to every Nth warp end as a guide marker helps track the repeat during pickup.
